CLARITY.justification: The paper is well-organized with a clear structure (Introduction → Problem & Approach → NPI background → recursive formulations → experiments → conclusion) and the main contribution—introducing recursion to obtain provable generalization for neural programs—is stated early in the Introduction. Notation is mostly consistent and the inclusion of Algorithms 1–4 and detailed appendices aids understanding, though a few notational shortcuts/omissions (e.g., the pt/kt program-key discussion and occasional footnote references) could be clarified. Figures and tables are referenced and the trace examples and pseudocode are informative, but the provided text shows missing or duplicated fragments (e.g., repeated paragraphs at the end) and a few minor editorial/grammatical artifacts that slightly hinder readability.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper advances a clear, novel perspective by explicitly incorporating recursion into neural programming architectures and using the recursion abstraction to enable finite verification (‘‘This paper is the first (to our knowledge) to investigate... provable generalization properties’’; Sec. 1 and 2.2). The technical contribution is straightforward—modifying NPI training traces to include recursive calls (Sec. 3.2)—but non-trivial in effect, because it yields provable perfect generalization via testing base cases and reduction rules and is demonstrated across several tasks (addition, bubble sort, topological sort, quicksort). This is a notable and well-differentiated contribution relative to prior neural-programming work, though the mechanism reuses existing NPI capabilities rather than introducing a wholly new model, so it is not a radical methodological leap.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper clearly states and supports its main technical claim that adding recursion to an NPI-style architecture enables finite verification and (in practice) perfect generalization: it gives a formal statement and a verification procedure (Sec. 3.3), shows how recursion is implemented via trace changes and LSTM reset at calls (Sec. 3.1–3.2), and reports empirical perfect/generalization results on addition, bubble sort, topological sort and quicksort (Sec. 4). The remaining concerns are that the correctness of the proof strategy depends critically on constructing a complete set V of step-inputs, and the authors admit that for several tasks V was obtained or validated empirically/randomly (Sec. 3.3 and A.6) or required environment simplifications (bubble sort), so completeness of V is not always strictly guaranteed. Experimental methodology also lacks statistical details and stronger baselines, but these issues are minor relative to the core technical contribution and do not invalidate the central claims.
